antipattern.symptom: Every change touches 3+ folders (models/, views/, serializers/, tests/). Code review takes hours because the diff is scattered.
antipattern.root-cause: Project uses flat global folders (models/, views/, serializers/) instead of domain apps. The "modify one feature" workflow hits multiple sibling folders.
antipattern.fix: Migrate to apps/<domain>/ layout. Each domain owns its models.py / services.py / selectors.py / apis.py. Spec rule r1-apps-layout is the target.

antipattern.symptom: core/exceptions.py imports apps.users.models.User to raise UserNotFound. Circular ImportError appears on a partial-test run; only some test orderings trigger it.
antipattern.root-cause: Reverse dependency: core/ should be a sink, but it imports from apps/. Now load-order matters.
antipattern.fix: Refactor so the exception in core/ stays domain-agnostic; the apps/users/services.py raises it with a User reference. Rule r3-core-no-import-from-apps forbids reverse imports.

antipattern.symptom: services.py grows to 1200 lines. Half the functions are reads (user_list, order_get_by_id), half are writes (order_process). It's hard to spot the side-effecting code.
antipattern.root-cause: "services" became a catch-all. The reads/writes split signal is lost.
antipattern.fix: Split: selectors.py = pure reads (no side effects); services.py = writes (DB mutations, side effects). Function naming preserves the signal (entity_query vs entity_action).

antipattern.symptom: Imports include `apps.User` (singular), `apps.Orders` (PascalCase), and `apps.user_profile` (singular). Cross-app aliasing convention breaks: `user_models` vs `Users_models`.
antipattern.root-cause: App folders were created without naming discipline. Each contributor picks their own style.
antipattern.fix: Enforce snake_case plural nouns: users, orders, user_profiles. Rule r5-naming-conventions. Lint pass catches violations.
